title: Pilot

jokes.text: Opening scene subverts expectations: we think we're watching a football pep-talk, but the coach is yelling at cheerleaders while two teens are having an earnest romantic conversation in the bleachers, completely oblivious to the chaos around them.
jokes.setup: Aggressive coach bellowing 'COME ON LADIES, LET'S SEE SOME HUSTLE' — classic sports movie energy, suggests athletic intensity.
jokes.punchline: Cut to two teens doing a soap-opera level emotional check-in about their relationship ('I need you to talk to me') while athletic chaos surrounds them.
jokes.explanation: A smart cold-open misdirect that sets the show's thesis: emotional teen drama exists in total contrast to the performative macho world around it. The juxtaposition is funny and tonally efficient. Loses points because the joke is structural — it's more of a premise-setter than a laugh-out-loud moment.

jokes.text: Brett finally delivers his big emotional line — 'I love you SO much... it scares me' — after Ashley's impassioned speech. The line is so clichéd and overwrought that the beat after it lands as pure comedy.
jokes.setup: Ashley delivers an earnest, sincere emotional plea for communication and openness.
jokes.punchline: Brett's response is the most Hollywood-romance cliché possible: 'It scares me.' Delivered with complete sincerity while cheerleaders run drills behind them.
jokes.explanation: The gap between the earnestness of the line and the absurdity of the setting does the work here. It's a subtle joke — the show is gently mocking teen romantic melodrama without being cruel. Functional but not exceptional.

jokes.text: The freaks' conversation about the Molly Hatchett shirt with the executioner and severed head — described in loving detail — being rejected at church.
jokes.setup: A burnout kid describes his metal shirt — executioner, bloody ax, severed head — with pride.
jokes.punchline: The priest turned him away from church for wearing it.
jokes.explanation: Good character establishment for the freaks — the specificity of the shirt description is funny, and the church rejection is a solid small payoff. The real joke is that this kid is genuinely surprised this happened.

jokes.text: 'They're supposed to FORGIVE people there. So you hate my shirt. FORGIVE ME — let me come in.'
jokes.setup: Daniel explains he was rejected from church for his death-metal shirt.
jokes.punchline: He applies church doctrine of forgiveness as a literal argument for why they should let him in with the shirt — deadpan logical absurdism.
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely sharp. Daniel applies scripture's own logic — forgiveness — as a legal argument for his right to attend church in a death-metal shirt. It's character-perfect (the burnout who is actually smarter than he looks), theologically coherent in its own absurd way, and economically delivered. One of the best jokes in the episode. Comes close to 8-range but stops just short because it's a single beat rather than a sustained comedic construction.

jokes.text: 'I believe in God, man. I've seen him. I've felt his power. He plays drums for Led Zeppelin, and his name is John Bonham, baby!'
jokes.setup: The conversation about church and God takes a sincere turn as Nick claims genuine faith.
jokes.punchline: Nick's God is John Bonham, Led Zeppelin's drummer. Delivered with complete religious conviction.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. The line works on multiple levels: it perfectly encapsulates who Nick is (a true believer in rock and roll as religion), it's theologically absurdist, it's quotable, and the delivery — 'baby!' — sells the genuine devotion. The callback later in the episode to Nick's drum kit retroactively makes this even funnier. Choosing John Bonham specifically (not just 'a drummer') is a precision joke. This justifies the 8+ score: it's character-defining, original, well-structured, and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Sam doing his Bill Murray Caddyshack impression ('Here's those noogies you ordered') and getting socially destroyed for it by older kid Alan.
jokes.setup: Sam does an enthusiastic, earnest Bill Murray impression for what he believes is an appreciative audience.
jokes.punchline: Alan immediately turns it against him: 'Bill Murray sucks. What is he, your boyfriend? Sam QUEER.'
jokes.explanation: The cringe comedy of Sam's enthusiasm being publicly humiliated is well-executed. The show is honest about how painfully this kind of thing lands at 14. The homophobic insult from Alan is period-accurate but designed to make the audience uncomfortable, not laugh — which is the point. Cringe comedy at its most grounded.

jokes.text: Lindsay steps in and offers to fight Alan herself. Alan's friends warn him: 'Watch out, Alan. I think she's HIGH ON POT.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay coolly defuses a bully situation by threatening Alan back, putting him on the defensive.
jokes.punchline: Alan's friend's panicked warning is 'I think she's high on pot' — as if that's the most dangerous thing possible.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because 1980 suburban terror hierarchy is perfectly captured — being 'high on pot' made someone genuinely unpredictable and dangerous in the teenage imagination. Lindsay's follow-up ('Yeah. I might just go psycho on you') leans into it beautifully.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Yeah. I might just go psycho on you.'
jokes.setup: Alan's friend has just warned him Lindsay might be high.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay plays into the fear with total deadpan cool, neither confirming nor denying — she just lets the threat hang there.
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy. Lindsay doesn't refute the drug accusation or defend herself — she weaponizes it. It's the funniest possible response and says everything about who Lindsay is becoming: someone learning to use the freak reputation strategically. The line is short and lands with real force.

jokes.text: Sam tells Lindsay she didn't need to help him. Lindsay says she knows. Then: 'And by the way, I weigh 103 pounds.'
jokes.setup: Alan said Sam weighs less than 100 pounds as an insult. Sam thanks Lindsay but says he could handle it.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's only correction to the whole situation: she doesn't weigh less than 100 pounds. She weighs 103. That's her priority.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. The entire bully confrontation gets deflated to this: Lindsay, who just absolutely faced down a larger bully, only cares about the '100 pounds' detail being wrong by 3 pounds. It's perfectly observed — teenage vanity transcends heroism. Economy is nearly perfect; every word earns its place.

jokes.text: Sam, after the bully encounter: 'Man, I hate high school.'
jokes.setup: Sam has just been humiliated, threatened, and then rescued by his sister.
jokes.punchline: The most understated possible summary of everything that just happened.
jokes.explanation: It's a thesis-statement joke — simultaneously the funniest possible summary of the scene AND the show's premise. Not original as a sentiment, but the timing and delivery make it land. Its quotability comes from being universally relatable.

jokes.text: Harold Weir's cautionary tale about smoking: 'I had a friend that used to smoke. You know what he's doing now? He's dead. You think smoking makes you look cool? Let's go dig him up now and see how cool he looks.'
jokes.setup: Harold tries to warn Lindsay about smoking with a story about a friend.
jokes.punchline: 'Let's go dig him up now and see how cool he looks.' — Harold doesn't realize how insane this sounds as a parenting technique.
jokes.explanation: Harold Weir as a character is almost entirely defined by his catastrophizing cautionary tales, and this is the purest expression of it in the pilot. 'Let's go dig him up and see how cool he looks' is a spectacular line — dark, absurd, completely sincere from Harold's perspective, and perfectly structured. The escalation from 'he's dead' to the exhumation offer is the joke. Genuinely great character comedy that borders on the 8-range anchor.

jokes.text: Sam announces he and his friends are going to watch Monty Python and the Holy Grail twice in a row Saturday night instead of going to the homecoming dance.
jokes.setup: Parents are pushing the kids to attend the homecoming dance.
jokes.punchline: Sam's alternative plan — seeing Holy Grail twice in a row — is presented as obviously superior, with complete sincerity.
jokes.explanation: Good character moment — instantly establishes the geeks' social priorities. The specificity of Holy Grail (not just 'a movie') is accurate to nerd culture of the era. Doesn't quite land as a standalone joke but works as world-building comedy.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'They're just a chance for the popular kids to experiment with sex in their cars.'
jokes.setup: Harold is arguing that high school dances are about socializing.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's bluntly cynical deconstruction of what homecoming dances are 'actually' about — delivered at the dinner table.
jokes.explanation: Sharp dinner-table comedy. Lindsay's deadpan cynicism cuts through Harold's nostalgia, and the specificity ('in their cars') grounds it in real 1980 teen behavior. Jean's horrified non-response is implied and funny.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'If that's what you want me to do, then I'd be happy to go.' [implying she'd experiment with sex in her car at the dance]
jokes.setup: Harold panics at Lindsay's 'sex in cars' comment and tells her to go.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay deadpans that she'd be 'happy to go' — knowing exactly what she's implying and making Harold panic further.
jokes.explanation: Quick callback that shows Lindsay's wit. She baits her father and then immediately springs the trap. It's economical and shows she's smarter than the adults give her credit for.

jokes.text: Harold: 'There was a girl in our school. She had premarital sex. You know what she did on graduation day? Died... of an overdose. Heroin.'
jokes.setup: Harold is warning Lindsay about the dangers of homecoming dances, apparently.
jokes.punchline: The logical leap from 'premarital sex' to 'heroin overdose on graduation day' is so catastrophically non-linear that it becomes a character-defining joke.
jokes.explanation: Harold's cautionary tale formula is perfected here. The beat before 'Died...' is brilliantly structured — you think the punchline is death, but then 'of an overdose. Heroin.' escalates it. The utter logical disconnect (sex → heroin death on graduation day) is both funnier than anything realistic and also a perfect portrait of 1980 parental panic culture. This justifies 8+: the character integration is exceptional (no other character on TV tells stories this way), the structure has a double-reveal, and it sets up Sam's comeback perfectly.

jokes.text: Sam: 'Dad, are any of your friends alive?' Harold: 'The smart ones.'
jokes.setup: Harold has now told multiple stories about friends who died from various cautionary failures.
jokes.punchline: Sam finally calls it out, and Harold's response ('The smart ones') is both defensive AND confirms he has a lot of dead friends.
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode. Sam's question is the perfect setup and Harold's response — 'The smart ones' — is a masterclass in economy. The joke works on every level: it acknowledges the running gag (Harold's friends keep dying in the stories), Harold's response is completely sincere while being absurdly revealing (he has many dead friends), and it pays off the callback structure perfectly. Two words. Maximum funny per word. This is the closest thing in the episode to a genuine standout moment. Absolutely justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Harold's 'midnight madness deer hunting sale' as the alternative to the homecoming dance: 'You can come wait on all those nice hunters.'
jokes.setup: Harold frames the homecoming dance vs. working his store as a choice.
jokes.punchline: 'Nice hunters' — the casualness of this as a legitimate alternative is the joke. Also, 'midnight madness deer hunting sale' as a phrase is inherently funny.
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'midnight madness deer hunting sale' does a lot of work establishing Harold as a character. The 'nice hunters' line has a deadpan quality — he genuinely sees this as a reasonable Saturday night for a teenager.

jokes.text: The geeks' guidance counselor Mr. Schweiber ('it's SCHWEIBER') — a student calls him 'Mr. K.' and he's been reduced to correcting his own name.
jokes.setup: Sam greets the guidance counselor cheerfully as 'Mr. K.'
jokes.punchline: The counselor's entire response is the gentle, tired correction of his own name — a man so ground down he introduces himself by correcting a mispronunciation.
jokes.explanation: Quick but effective — establishes the counselor as a man who has given up on most things except the pronunciation of his own name. Good background character work.

jokes.text: Someone uses Eli (a special-needs student) as a prank on a fellow student — 'Hey, Eli. I think Tom needs a hug.' Eli approaches and hugs Tom, who is visibly horrified.
jokes.setup: Eli is established as friendly and enthusiastic. A student weaponizes this against Tom.
jokes.punchline: Tom's 'You're such a jerk, man' and the laughter — the prank works, Eli is oblivious, and everyone involved feels slightly bad about laughing.
jokes.explanation: This moment introduces Eli in a way that's somewhat uncomfortable — he's being used as a prop for a prank. The show knows this is wrong (Tom calls it out), but it's still trading on the joke of the unwanted hug. Functional but not craft comedy; it's also setup for later Eli material which recontextualizes it. Scored relatively low because the joke relies on the exploitation dynamic rather than wit.

jokes.text: Mr. Schweiber does a William Shatner impression of Captain Kirk, calls the cafeteria 'the Enterprise mess hall,' and says he's 'so hungry I could eat a Tribble.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Schweiber approaches the geek table to bond with students over shared interests.
jokes.punchline: His Kirk impression and 'could eat a Tribble' line is so desperately try-hard — a middle-aged man performing geek fluency for teenagers who aren't impressed — that it becomes both cringe comedy and character portrait.
jokes.explanation: The joke works because Mr. Schweiber is clearly performing his geekiness rather than living it — and the geeks see right through it. 'Could eat a Tribble' is a well-chosen detail: obscure enough to be nerdy, weird enough to be funny, and delivered with zero awareness of how it reads.

jokes.text: Neal doesn't get the Kirk impression and asks 'Who's that?' Bill answers: 'John Wayne.' Neal: 'Oh. OK.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Schweiber delivers a Shatner/Kirk impression expecting the geeks to appreciate it.
jokes.punchline: The geeks don't even recognize it. Bill's confident mislabel ('John Wayne') and Neal's complete acceptance of that answer is funnier than any recognition would have been.
jokes.explanation: Beautifully constructed two-beat joke. Bill's confident wrong answer ('John Wayne') and Neal's total acceptance of it subverts every expectation — these are supposed to be the geeks who would know Star Trek. The economy is near-perfect. The joke also subtly establishes Bill as someone who states wrong things with conviction, a character trait that pays off throughout the series.

jokes.text: Neal complains his mom puts a note in his lunch. Bill responds: 'At least your mom doesn't write the note ON THE BAG.'
jokes.setup: Neal says his mom putting a note in his lunch is embarrassing.
jokes.punchline: Bill one-ups the embarrassment: his mom writes the note directly on the bag — no note, just the bag itself being the message.
jokes.explanation: Clean escalation joke. The specificity of 'on the bag' elevates this from a standard mommy-is-embarrassing complaint to something genuinely specific and visual. Bill saying it without self-pity — just as a fact — makes it funnier than if he'd played for sympathy.

jokes.text: Sam reports to Mr. Kowchevski that Alan smashed his Twinkies. Mr. Kowchevski's response: 'Oh, my God. You're such a woman.'
jokes.setup: Sam, after being told to 'be a man,' immediately tattles to a teacher about the Twinkies.
jokes.punchline: The teacher's response is more derisive than the bully's was. The adult who should help him adds to the humiliation.
jokes.explanation: The darkly funny truth here: the adults are no help to Sam, and the system that's supposed to protect him just extends the humiliation. Mr. Kowchevski is funnier for then immediately making Alan buy new Twinkies — authority is arbitrary and contradictory.

jokes.text: Alan's explanation to Mr. Kowchevski for crushing Sam's Twinkies: 'I was just bending down to talk to him, and I accidentally leaned on it. I'm sorry.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Kowchevski asks Alan to explain himself.
jokes.punchline: Alan's transparently false excuse — delivered with butter-won't-melt sincerity — and the teacher's decision to accept it and make him buy new ones anyway.
jokes.explanation: Standard bully-excuse comedy, elevated slightly by how brazenly implausible Alan's story is and how the teacher half-buys it anyway. Functional but not noteworthy.

jokes.text: Mr. Kowchevski's parting advice to Sam after the whole Twinkie saga: 'Sam, you want some advice? Be a man.'
jokes.setup: The teacher has just handled the bully situation, then gives Sam personal advice.
jokes.punchline: The advice is 'be a man' — the same hollow instruction that led to Sam getting humiliated in the first place. The teacher offers no actual help, just patriarchal platitude.
jokes.explanation: Works as institutional satire — the adult authority figure's idea of mentorship is a useless cliché. The joke is that this teacher thinks 'be a man' is profound advice. It's not a big laugh but it's honest and contributes to the episode's theme.

jokes.text: A freak grabs Lindsay's butt on the smoking patio. His justification: 'Your butt was calling to me.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay nervously arrives at the freaks' smoking patio for the first time.
jokes.punchline: She's immediately assaulted, and Ken's response is this — delivered without a shred of self-awareness or apology.
jokes.explanation: Period-accurate but uncomfortable in a way the show is clearly aware of. Ken's total unselfconsciousness about it is the character joke — he's not malicious, just genuinely oblivious. The cringe lands, though the joke itself isn't particularly crafted.

jokes.text: Lindsay is identified by a freak as 'that chick who got an A.' Lindsay: 'Yeah, well... what are you gonna do?' The freak replies: 'I don't know. What ARE you gonna do?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is being assessed by the freaks on the patio. Getting an A is her only notable quality to them.
jokes.punchline: The conversation immediately stalls because neither person has anything to say. The repetition of 'what are you gonna do' fills the silence until it becomes uncomfortable and funny.
jokes.explanation: Naturalistic awkward comedy that Freaks and Geeks excels at. The exchange perfectly captures the social gap between Lindsay and the freaks — she can't code-switch yet, doesn't know how to make small talk in this world, and the A is genuinely all they have to discuss. The repetition of the phrase is the joke.

jokes.text: Lindsay nervously asks if the freaks are going to the homecoming dance. Reaction: 'I beg your pardon? ... That's funny. It's a joke, right?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay tries to find common ground with the freaks by mentioning the homecoming dance.
jokes.punchline: The freaks react as if she's just proposed something deeply surreal — the dance is so far outside their world that it reads as absurdist comedy.
jokes.explanation: The reaction is the joke — 'I beg your pardon?' from a burnout is perfect. Shows the social gulf between Lindsay's world and theirs in one beat.

jokes.text: Nick: 'My dad's making you go to the dance? Man, what's that all about? Who's your dad — Hitler?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay explains her dad is making her go to the dance.
jokes.punchline: Nick's hyperbole: forcing your kid to attend a school dance = Hitler.
jokes.explanation: Classic teenage hyperbole — any minor parental restriction becomes fascism. Nick's earnestness makes it funnier than if he'd said it ironically. Solid but not memorable.

jokes.text: Lindsay tries to salvage the homecoming dance idea: 'I just thought maybe you guys were gonna go... to make fun of people. That could be kinda funny, right?'
jokes.setup: The freaks have rejected the homecoming dance as beneath them.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's desperate pivot — maybe if we frame it as ironic mockery? — reveals how hard she's trying to fit in and how she doesn't quite understand who these people are yet.
jokes.explanation: This is Lindsay's most honest and funny moment in the patio scene. Her code-switching attempt fails in real time — she offers mockery as the price of admission and still gets rejected. Very true to the experience of trying to join a new social group.

jokes.text: Nick's reason for skipping the dance: 'My cousin just sent me a bunch of mushrooms, and I'm gonna eat them.'
jokes.setup: Nick explains why he has a 'prior engagement' that prevents him from attending the homecoming dance.
jokes.punchline: The prior engagement is eating psychedelic mushrooms. Delivered with the same gravity as a business meeting.
jokes.explanation: The formal framing ('prior engagement') combined with the casual revelation of drug consumption is the joke. Nick doesn't frame it as rebellion — it's just his Saturday night. Character-specific and period-accurate.

jokes.text: The freaks' disco aversion: 'I'd rather make out with Principal Farber.' — followed by a reaction sound suggesting Farber is deeply unappealing.
jokes.setup: Discussion of why homecoming is terrible: they'd play disco.
jokes.punchline: Making out with the principal is preferable to disco. The implied ugliness of Principal Farber makes the hyperbole land.
jokes.explanation: Standard 'anything is better than [thing]' joke elevated slightly by the specificity of Principal Farber as a disgusting option. Works as character comedy for Ken.

jokes.text: Millie interrupts Lindsay on the smoking patio to tell her about the Academic Decathlon deadline: 'But you're our best Math-lete!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay is trying to establish herself with the freaks on the smoking patio.
jokes.punchline: Her wholesome academic-world friend shows up and yells 'Math-lete' loud enough for all the freaks to hear.
jokes.explanation: The social humiliation is specific and real — having your nerd credentials publicly announced in front of the cool kids you're trying to impress is a particular kind of high school nightmare. Millie's complete unawareness of what she's doing makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Neal's solution to the Alan problem: 'Listen to Neal. You should handle Alan the same way Han Solo dealt with Jabba the Hut. Avoid him. Just avoid him forever?' 'Just for 4 years.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are trying to strategize around their bully problem.
jokes.punchline: Neal's Han Solo analogy is for avoidance, not confrontation — and his timeframe of 'just 4 years' (i.e., all of high school) is presented as perfectly reasonable.
jokes.explanation: The Star Wars reference is used subversively — Han Solo is the coolest possible frame, but the advice it yields is to hide for four years. Neal delivers this as if it's brilliant strategy. 'Just for 4 years' is the perfect capper. Good economy and strong character-specific joke.

jokes.text: Cindy Sanders returns Sam's jacket she found in science class. Neal's elaborate decoding: 'She saw it, she remembered it was yours, she picked it up and carried it through the halls... Cindy Sanders is IN LOVE with you, man.'
jokes.setup: Cindy returns Sam's jacket as a friendly gesture.
jokes.punchline: Neal's forensic analysis of the act concludes with 'Cindy Sanders is in love with you, man' — an enormous leap from 'she returned your lost jacket.'
jokes.explanation: Classic teenage male logic — a girl was polite, therefore she's in love. Neal's escalating analysis ('People probably saw her with it and everything') is perfectly observed. The joke is both in the conclusion and the absurd seriousness with which Neal presents his evidence.

jokes.text: Bill's counter-argument: 'She's a cheerleader. You've seen Star Wars 27 times. Do the math.'
jokes.setup: Sam is being talked into asking Cindy to the dance after the jacket incident.
jokes.punchline: Bill's blunt reality check is delivered with complete matter-of-factness: 27 Star Wars viewings vs. cheerleader = incompatible.
jokes.explanation: Great Bill moment. The '27 times' is a beautifully specific number — not 'a lot of times,' but 27, suggesting someone actually counted. Bill's deadpan delivery of an objectively brutal truth, without cruelty, is perfectly in character. 'Do the math' as punctuation is economical and funny. Justifies the high character-integration score — this is distinctly Bill's voice.

jokes.text: Sam's dad apparently told him 'the prettiest girls usually don't get asked out because guys are too afraid to ask them.' Neal and Bill's reactions: 'Really? Maybe I should ask out Farrah Fawcett-Majors. Hey, maybe I should ask out BILL'S MOM.'
jokes.setup: Sam invokes his dad's dating advice as if it's profound strategy.
jokes.punchline: Neal immediately applies the logic to Farrah Fawcett. Bill immediately applies it to his own mom. 'You better not.'
jokes.explanation: Two-step escalation that works perfectly: Farrah Fawcett is the logical application of 'too pretty to get asked,' then Bill's mom is the comic escalation — and Bill's 'You better not' response suggests this is a running vulnerability. The fact that Bill's mom becomes a recurring joke in the series makes this a great pilot establishment.

jokes.text: Immediately after the Farrah Fawcett joke, Bill adds: 'Hey, maybe I should ask out Bill's mom.' Sam: 'You better not.'
jokes.setup: Neal has just made the Farrah Fawcett escalation.
jokes.punchline: Neal immediately escalates to Bill's mom as the comparable unattainable — and Bill's injured 'You better not' reveals this is a real sore point, implying either (a) this joke has been made before or (b) Bill's mom is notably attractive, which is a whole thing.
jokes.explanation: Bill's 'You better not' is the punchline — the implication that there's history here, that Bill's mom is genuinely considered attractive by these kids, and Bill knows it and doesn't like it. The rewatch bonus is for knowing Bill's mom becomes a more developed character element in the series. Quick and character-specific.

jokes.text: Daniel to the person who stole an exam: 'Where are the answers?' 'You just said to steal the test.' 'Oh, good work, genius. Way to use your brain.'
jokes.setup: Someone was sent to steal a chemistry exam and returns with just the test.
jokes.punchline: Daniel's completely reasonable frustration that the person didn't steal the answer key too — while simultaneously acknowledging the entire mission was to cheat.
jokes.explanation: The absurd logic of being called 'genius' for only half-executing a theft is funny. 'Way to use your brain' as sarcasm about someone who demonstrably didn't is competent irony.

jokes.text: Nick, asked for gum, pulls out used gum. Kim: 'Oh, yeah, that's real great. Why don't you blow your nose in some bread and make me a sandwich, too?'
jokes.setup: Kim asks for gum. Nick produces something inappropriate (implied used gum).
jokes.punchline: Kim's insult is gloriously specific: 'blow your nose in some bread and make me a sandwich.'
jokes.explanation: Kim's insult is a standout line in the episode. 'Blow your nose in some bread and make me a sandwich' is original, disgusting, specific, and perfectly in Kim's voice — she doesn't do generic insults, she constructs visceral ones. This is the first glimpse of Kim as a character rather than just 'the mean girl,' and it's a great one. Justifies 8+: the image is original, the structure (parallel: gross item → sandwich) is tight, and it's clearly character-specific.

jokes.text: Kim to Lindsay: 'What, are you doing her so that she'll help you with your math homework?' — accusing Daniel of using Lindsay sexually for academic gain.
jokes.setup: Kim is hostile to Lindsay joining the group. She confronts Daniel about it.
jokes.punchline: Her specific theory — that Daniel is sleeping with Lindsay to get math tutoring — says more about Kim and Daniel's relationship than it does about Lindsay.
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the specificity of 'math homework' — Kim's theory is simultaneously insulting to both Daniel and Lindsay in different ways. It's a good character moment for Kim.

jokes.text: Kim to Lindsay: 'Hey brain — I shoplift in your daddy's store.'
jokes.setup: Kim is attacking Lindsay's social standing.
jokes.punchline: Rather than an insult about Lindsay personally, Kim reveals she steals from her family's business — bragging about criminality as a status move.
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment. Kim's status attack isn't an insult — it's a confession. She steals from Lindsay's family as a power statement. The casual bragging about theft as social dominance is both funny and revealing. Economy is tight: 'your daddy's store' does a lot of condescension work.

jokes.text: Lindsay attempts to defuse Kim: 'I'm sorry. Let's be friends.' She extends her hand. Shakes. 'There. Now we're friends. See you at the mall.'
jokes.setup: Kim has been relentlessly hostile to Lindsay. Lindsay tries to end it.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's resolution attempt — performative friendliness, a handshake, 'See you at the mall' — is so absurd in context that it works as pure deadpan comedy. The casualness of 'see you at the mall' is the kicker.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's 'See you at the mall' is a small masterpiece of deadpan. She performs the entire arc of conflict-to-friendship in about four seconds, knowing Kim won't accept it, and exits anyway. The joke is in its audacity and in how thoroughly Kim ignores it.

jokes.text: After Kim's extended hostile rant: 'That is exactly why I don't carry a purse.'
jokes.setup: Kim has just delivered a long, passionate tirade about Lindsay's intrusion into the group.
jokes.punchline: Ken's non-sequitur response implies Kim's purse (presumably full of whatever Kim keeps in purses) is the reason he avoids the whole scene — complete tonal deflation.
jokes.explanation: This is the best single-line joke in the episode and one of the great throwaway lines in the pilot season. Ken's response to Kim's intense emotional speech is a non-sequitur about purses — delivered completely deadpan, as if it's the logical conclusion of everything she said. It works on every level: it deflates Kim's drama, it establishes Ken's character as someone who responds to intensity with absurdist calm, it's completely unpredictable, and it's structurally perfect (timing, brevity, non-sequitur logic). The economy score is nearly perfect — four words do enormous comedic work. Absolutely justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Daniel's reason he has to leave the confrontation: 'I gotta get to shop class. It's the only one I can pass.'
jokes.setup: The social standoff is resolved and people need to disperse.
jokes.punchline: Daniel's exit is to shop class — and the reason he specifies is that it's the only class he can pass. He says this without shame.
jokes.explanation: The total absence of embarrassment in Daniel's admission is the joke. He's not complaining about his academic record — it's just a fact he states for logistical reasons. Good character economy.

jokes.text: Lindsay asks 'These shorts aren't very flattering, are they?' Nick: 'No, you look good.' She smiles, walks off. Reaction beat: Nick watches her go.
jokes.setup: Lindsay is wearing unflattering gym shorts and asks for honest feedback.
jokes.punchline: Nick's answer ('No, you look good') is slightly confused — he answers the first sentence with the second sentence's answer — and the moment reads as his first flicker of attraction to Lindsay. The audience realizes this before she does.
jokes.explanation: A soft, warm joke that's more character beat than laugh — Nick's slightly confused answer ('No, you look good') doesn't answer what she asked, but it's sweet. The humor comes from his awkwardness around attraction, establishing a dynamic that runs through the season.

jokes.text: Sam to himself, after being threatened by Alan in a new location: 'I kind of wish I didn't come to school today.'
jokes.setup: Sam has had an unrelenting series of humiliations throughout the day.
jokes.punchline: The understatement of 'kind of wish' after everything that's happened is the joke — it's the mildest possible response to a truly terrible day.
jokes.explanation: Mirrors joke 10 ('I hate high school') structurally — another Sam understatement beat. Slightly weaker because it's a reprise of the same formula, but 'kind of' is perfectly chosen.

jokes.text: Sam screws up his courage and awkwardly asks a girl to the homecoming dance. She already has a date. He immediately tries another girl. Laughter in response.
jokes.setup: Sam is trying to get a date to the homecoming dance by approaching random girls.
jokes.punchline: The first rejection is immediate; the pivot to the second attempt is so quick it becomes its own joke — and the second girl laughs at him.
jokes.explanation: Standard awkward-teen-gets-rejected beat. The double rejection in quick succession escalates it slightly. Not particularly inventive but earns its cringe.

jokes.text: Sam tries to talk to Eli (the intellectually disabled student) who is with the mean girl Kim. The whole sequence where Kim manipulates the situation — 'Do you LOVE me?' — building to Eli being taunted into asking the girl to the dance.
jokes.setup: Sam is looking for Neal, accidentally makes eye contact with Kim, who weaponizes Eli against him.
jokes.punchline: Kim engineers a scenario where the intellectually disabled Eli is enthusiastically offering to be the mean girl's homecoming date, and Sam is caught in the middle looking like he set it up.
jokes.explanation: The extended awkwardness of Kim weaponizing Eli is uncomfortable rather than funny — the show is walking a careful line here. The cringe is the comedy, and it works because the show treats Eli with dignity even while the situation is absurd.

jokes.text: Gym teacher announces the day off for homecoming: 'We're gonna do something fun... DODGE BALL.' Geeks' synchronized look of horror.
jokes.setup: The coach builds to a big announcement about a fun day off.
jokes.punchline: Dodge ball. The geeks' reaction (implied sighs, dread) is the second beat of the joke.
jokes.explanation: Classic setup-reveal joke — the 'fun' alternative is the one thing worse than class for the geeks. Structurally clean. The geeks' reaction ('Oh no') is the real punchline.

jokes.text: The geeks' dodge ball strategy discussion: 'If we just let ourselves get hit, then we can get out.' 'Let's just pretend to get hit. Nobody'd know.' 'I don't think we can.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are trapped in dodge ball and trying to survive.
jokes.punchline: Their solution — theatrical fake-outs — is both cowardly and impractical, and Bill's 'I don't think we can' (fake convincingly) delivers the coup de grâce.
jokes.explanation: Three-beat joke that escalates logically: plan → implementation question → fatal flaw. Bill's final answer acknowledges they're bad at performing anything, including being hit. Character-specific and well-structured.

jokes.text: Coach to the geeks cowering at the edge of the dodge ball court: 'Ladies, put down the mascara, get in there and play.'
jokes.setup: The geeks are clearly not participating in dodge ball.
jokes.punchline: 'Put down the mascara' — the coach's insult implies they're doing their makeup instead of playing, which is both absurd and period-accurate coach-speak.
jokes.explanation: Functional coach-bully humor. 'Mascara' is a specific enough image to be funny. The coach is a consistent source of period-appropriate toxic masculinity played for cringe comedy.

jokes.text: Alan to Sam during dodge ball: 'Look! Little Man's making a run for it!' Sam: 'Hey! Only MY MOM'S allowed to call me that!'
jokes.setup: Alan taunts Sam with a nickname during dodge ball.
jokes.punchline: Sam's indignant response reveals his mom calls him 'Little Man' — making the vulnerability much worse. He's not objecting to the insult, just the copyright.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. Sam's protest accidentally reveals that his mom calls him 'Little Man' — which is so much worse than Alan calling him that. The joke works because Sam doesn't realize what he's admitted. His logic (only mom gets to use that nickname) is completely sincere and completely self-defeating. The 'copyright' framing of a nickname is original and the economy is tight. Justifies 8+: it's character-specific, structurally clever (the protest creates a worse revelation), original, and highly quotable.

jokes.text: The dodge ball sequence: a large kid hurls balls at the geeks with full athletic intensity, sending them flying. Pure physical slapstick — the geeks getting absolutely destroyed.
jokes.setup: The geeks are trying to survive dodge ball. An enormous kid is launched at them.
jokes.punchline: The size disparity is the joke — these small nerds being used as human targets by a much larger, genuinely athletic kid. The visceral impact of the throws is played straight and therefore funnier.
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy that works better on screen than on the page. The sequence is energetic and the size mismatch is a reliable source of slapstick. The coach's refereeing ('That's illegal, you're out of there') while doing nothing to actually protect the kids is a good background joke.

jokes.text: Sam, alone on the dodge ball court facing the big kid, lowers his ball: 'Dodge ball's kind of a stupid game, isn't it?' Big kid: 'You just signed your death warrant.'
jokes.setup: Sam is the last geek standing in a one-vs-one dodge ball standoff against a much larger opponent.
jokes.punchline: Sam's response to mortal athletic danger is a philosophical observation about the game itself — delivered with genuine calm. The big kid's reaction ('death warrant') is the comic confirmation.
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke. Sam's calm, genuine observation about dodge ball being 'kind of stupid' in the middle of being hunted by a large child is a perfectly Sam Weir moment — his intellectual response to physical threat. It's unexpected, character-specific, and economically perfect. The big kid's 'You just signed your death warrant' — using formal, theatrical language for a gym class situation — escalates it. Justifies 8+: character integration is exceptional (Sam's response is uniquely his), the structure contrasts perfectly (philosophical vs. physical aggression), and it's quotable.

jokes.text: The geeks asking Eli political questions: 'That Jimmy Carter — he's some president, right? He's been doing a helluva job.' Leading to 'I bet you'd make a good president, Eli.' 'PRESIDENT ELI!'
jokes.setup: Some boys are engaging Eli in fake political conversation, knowing he can't fully participate.
jokes.punchline: Eli's genuine enthusiasm for 'President Eli' as a concept — and the escalating chanting — reveals the boys' casual cruelty AND Eli's dignified engagement with the idea.
jokes.explanation: The episode's most ethically complicated comedy beat. The laughing-at vs. laughing-with question is present throughout, and Lindsay's intervention cuts through it. The comedy is real but uncomfortable in a way that's clearly intentional.

jokes.text: Lindsay tells the boys to stop, explaining to Eli: 'They're only laughing at you because you're retarded.' Eli: 'I'm not retarded. I'm special.' Lindsay: 'I'm sorry. I know you're special.' Eli: 'I'm not retarded!' Lindsay: 'OK.' Boys: 'You're retarded!'
jokes.setup: Lindsay intervenes to protect Eli from being mocked.
jokes.punchline: Her intervention accidentally makes things worse — she uses the word Eli explicitly rejects, triggers him into a meltdown, and then the boys pile on with the word again. Her good intentions produce the worst possible outcome.
jokes.explanation: The irony is well-constructed: Lindsay's attempt to help triggers the crisis. The 'I'm special' / 'I know you're special' exchange is both sad and funny in the specific way Freaks and Geeks excels at — you're laughing and wincing at the same time. Eli's final line ('I'm not retarded, I AM special') delivered with genuine dignity is the real emotional anchor.

jokes.text: Eli's response: 'I'm not retarded. I'm special.' Then repeating: 'I'M SPECIAL.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just called Eli retarded in her attempt to defend him.
jokes.punchline: Eli's correction — 'I'm special' — is both his actual self-identification and a word that has its own set of complications, landing as genuinely funny and genuinely sad simultaneously. His insistence ('I'M SPECIAL') is heartbreaking in the best way.
jokes.explanation: Eli's correction plays on the specific late-'70s/early-'80s language shift from 'retarded' to 'special' — both terms are his actual vocabulary and both land with the same uncomfortable ambiguity. The show finds genuine comedy in the moment without exploiting Eli, because his self-assertion is presented with dignity. 'I'm special' as a statement of identity rather than irony is both funny and moving.

jokes.text: After Eli breaks his arm and Lindsay is sitting in the office: 'And I'm the mean one? How does that work?'
jokes.setup: Kim, who has been hostile to Lindsay all episode, watches as Lindsay's attempt at kindness results in Eli breaking his arm.
jokes.punchline: Kim's completely legitimate point — Lindsay's niceness caused more damage than Kim's hostility — lands as both funny and genuinely true.
jokes.explanation: Kim's line is a legitimate callback and a genuinely funny character moment. She's been positioned as the villain, but her observation is correct — and she's self-aware enough to notice the irony. 'How does that work?' as a capper is economical and sharply delivered. The line reframes Kim in one beat.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso asks Lindsay why she's not doing academic decathlon. Lindsay: 'Oh, God, please don't say that word.' Rosso: 'What word?' Lindsay: 'Math-lete.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Rosso pivots from the Eli crisis to Lindsay's academic decathlon status.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's visceral disgust at the word 'Math-lete' — the sound of it triggers her — is both funny and character-revealing.
jokes.explanation: Callback to Millie's earlier use of the same word. 'Math-lete' has become a running trigger for Lindsay, and her reaction to Rosso using it unprompted is funnier because she has to explain what word she means.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso tries to assess Lindsay's home situation: 'You havin' some problems at home?' 'No.' 'Is it a boy?' 'No.' 'Hey, you can tell me.'
jokes.setup: Rosso is attempting to connect with Lindsay as a guidance counselor.
jokes.punchline: His entire diagnostic checklist is two questions: home problems, boy. The vapidity of his counseling methodology is the joke.
jokes.explanation: Rosso as a character exists to be a well-meaning incompetent, and this sequence establishes that. His 'Is it a boy?' after 'problems at home?' perfectly summarizes 1980 guidance counseling.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso: 'From now on, I'm not Mr. Rosso, Guidance Counselor.' Lindsay: 'You're not?' Rosso: 'No. I'm just Jeff, your friend who cares.' ... 'Come on, Linds. Talk to your buddy Jeff.'
jokes.setup: Rosso decides to drop formal titles and connect with Lindsay 'as people.'
jokes.punchline: The performance of informality — naming himself, then calling himself her 'buddy' — is more awkward and formal than the counselor relationship it's replacing.
jokes.explanation: Rosso as the embodiment of 'trying too hard to connect with youth' is well-observed. 'Talk to your buddy Jeff' is a great line — the third-person self-reference while claiming informality is peak try-hard. The show understands that some adults performing youth-connection are performing their own needs more than actually helping.

jokes.text: Rosso asks how Lindsay is doing after everything. Lindsay: 'I've never felt better in all my life.' Rosso: 'Well, then maybe someone should tell your face.'
jokes.setup: Lindsay gives an obviously false answer to Rosso's caring inquiry.
jokes.punchline: 'Maybe someone should tell your face' — Rosso's one moment of actual perceptiveness, delivered as a soft joke.
jokes.explanation: The line is a slightly above-average 'your face says otherwise' joke. Rosso's unexpected perceptiveness after being played as clueless is a small character turn. Economy is good — the line is five words and earns its moment.

jokes.text: Mr. Schweiber's analysis of Alan: 'He's obviously acting out his insecurities in a new environment... In his own way, he's reaching out for a friend. Not that you should feel sorry for him, though. His kind should be destroyed.'
jokes.setup: Mr. Schweiber offers a compassionate psychological reading of Alan's bullying behavior.
jokes.punchline: The pivot from 'he's reaching out for a friend' to 'his kind should be destroyed' — delivered without pause — is a spectacular tonal shift.
jokes.explanation: One of the best jokes in the episode. The structure is formally elegant: compassionate buildup (full therapy-speak empathy for the bully) immediately reversed by 'his kind should be destroyed.' The tonal whiplash is the joke, and it tells you everything about Schweiber — he's performed the liberal-educator empathy routine but his actual instinct is annihilation. The phrase 'his kind' is also slightly absurd, elevating it from a simple 'destroy him' to something with a taxonomic chill. Absolutely justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Mr. Schweiber explains the 'Cressman Conundrum' — fight your bully and they'll leave you alone. 'Did it work?' 'He broke my tailbone, but the results were effective. He got expelled.'
jokes.setup: Schweiber presents his theory for handling bullies with academic authority.
jokes.punchline: 'He broke my tailbone, but the results were effective' — success is defined purely as 'the bully got expelled,' regardless of physical damage sustained.
jokes.explanation: The tailbone detail is specifically chosen — it's undignified in a way a broken arm isn't. Schweiber's framing of 'effective' despite a broken tailbone is perfect character work — he genuinely believes this strategy was sound. The logic that 'he got expelled' = victory even though you were hospitalized is great absurd math.

jokes.text: Neal reveals his bully-avoidance strategy: 'I know every route he takes to each class and I avoid those halls. Start leaving out the janitor's door like I do.' — immediately followed by him being found and threatened by a bully anyway.
jokes.setup: Neal advocates against fighting Alan, explaining his elaborate avoidance system.
jokes.punchline: Immediately after presenting his system as working, a bully appears and threatens him — demonstrating the system's failure in real time.
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic payoff — Neal's self-satisfied explanation of his avoidance system is immediately undercut by its failure. The timing (immediate after the boast) makes this structurally excellent. It's the show's cleanest setup-punchline in the episode's second half.

jokes.text: Kim spots Sam looking at her and calls him over: 'Hey, geek. You got a problem?' Sam: 'Uh, no. I was just looking at a friend of mine.' Kim: 'Are you telling me that I look like a friend of yours?'
jokes.setup: Sam stares at Kim, who is confrontational by nature.
jokes.punchline: Kim interprets 'friend' as an insult — the idea that she resembles someone Sam knows is an offense. Her worldview is so oppositional that even the word 'friend' becomes threatening.
jokes.explanation: Kim's hair-trigger hostility is well-established by now — the joke is that Sam can't say anything that won't land wrong. 'Are you telling me that I look like a friend of yours?' as an offense rather than a compliment reveals Kim's social worldview.

jokes.text: Kim's elaborate fake-out seduction of Sam: 'Is that true? Do you like me? Do you LOVE me?' 'I think you want to kiss me.' 'Come on. Just one little kiss. I'll be your girlfriend.'
jokes.setup: Kim has established she's hostile. She now begins a false seduction routine.
jokes.punchline: The escalation — from 'do you like me' to love to wanting to kiss to 'I'll be your girlfriend' — is an elaborate setup for a humiliation. Sam's paralyzed confusion throughout is the audience surrogate position.
jokes.explanation: The fake-seduction escalation is a well-structured cringe sequence. The audience knows where this is going and the comedy is in Sam's inability to escape or correctly read the situation. Effective but not especially inventive — this kind of scene exists in many teen comedies.

jokes.text: Kim slams Sam's face against the locker — 'In your dreams, geek.' The kiss fake-out complete.
jokes.setup: The entire fake seduction of Sam.
jokes.punchline: The locker slam and 'In your dreams, geek' — the reveal that all the romantic escalation was a setup for violence and humiliation.
jokes.explanation: The payoff to the fake seduction. Works well as a character moment for Kim but the structure is conventional — fake seduction leading to humiliation is a well-worn teen comedy beat. The 'In your dreams, geek' is stock dialogue. Impact is decent because the execution is committed.

jokes.text: Nick shows Lindsay his drum kit — a staggering setup of '14 mounted toms, 8 floor toms, 4 splashes, 2 gongs, 10 cowbells, 4 rides, 5 snares, a roto-tom rack, and a quadruple kick drum system.'
jokes.setup: Nick promises to show Lindsay something that will make her feel better about life.
jokes.punchline: The drum kit is so incomprehensibly large that it becomes a visual gag — 10 cowbells alone is an absurdist amount — and Nick's pride in it is complete and total.
jokes.explanation: The drum kit is a great visual joke that works as pure character integration — of course Nick's life passion is an absurdly large drum kit he probably can't fully play. '10 cowbells' is the number that pushes it into absurdist comedy. 'Be prepared to have your mind blown' followed by this was promised as a solution to Lindsay's existential crisis and delivered with complete sincerity.

jokes.text: Nick's life philosophy delivered to Lindsay as she faces the drum kit: 'You need to find your reason for living. You gotta find your big, gigantic drum kit, ya know?'
jokes.setup: Nick is trying to give Lindsay meaningful life advice.
jokes.punchline: The metaphor is literal — your 'drum kit' is a literal drum kit. Nick isn't using it as a metaphor; he genuinely believes a drum kit is the answer to existential crisis.
jokes.explanation: This is one of the best character-comedy moments in the pilot. Nick's self-help philosophy — find your passion, it saved me — is genuinely touching AND absurd because his passion is an impractical, enormous drum kit in his bedroom. 'You gotta find your big, gigantic drum kit' is a magnificent line because he means it as universal wisdom about purpose while Lindsay (and the audience) can see the literal insane drum kit he's pointing at. Calls back to John Bonham as God (joke 5) and completes Nick's character arc in the pilot. Justifies 8+: original framing, exceptional character integration, and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Lindsay's response to Nick's life-philosophy speech about finding your passion: 'Hey, maybe I'll buy a clarinet.' Nick: 'Yeah.'
jokes.setup: Nick has just delivered an earnest, passionate speech about finding your big gigantic drum kit as a metaphor for purpose.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's takeaway is: clarinet. Nick's 'Yeah' accepts this completely.
jokes.explanation: Two words ('Yeah' and 'clarinet') do extraordinary work. Lindsay has processed Nick's sprawling existential sermon and her response is the most modest, practical, unmotivated musical instrument possible. Nick's 'Yeah' — complete acceptance — is perfect because he can't distinguish between his drum-kit passion and a clarinet. The joke is about the gap between the speech and the response, and about Nick's inability to see the gap. Economy score is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: Mr. Rosso catches Lindsay and uses it as leverage: 'How about I call your parents and let them know their daughter's turning into a burnout who skips class? I can't make you be a math-lete, but I can make you work the refreshment table at the homecoming dance.'
jokes.setup: Rosso's 'let's rap as friends' approach has failed. He pivots to coercion.
jokes.punchline: The shift from 'I'm your buddy Jeff' to 'I'll call your parents' is a complete abandonment of the friend persona — and 'I can't make you be a math-lete' is a funny formal acknowledgment of his authority's limits before revealing what he can do.
jokes.explanation: The callback from Jeff-your-friend to authoritative-coercion is a good structural beat. Rosso's persona switch reveals that the friendship bit was always a tactic, not a genuine stance. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Harold: 'You know who used to cut class? Jimi Hendrix. You know what happened to him? He died, choking on his own vomit.'
jokes.setup: Harold finds out Lindsay skipped class and delivers his standard cautionary-tale format.
jokes.punchline: Jimi Hendrix as the 'cautionary tale about skipping class' example — ignoring decades of cultural reverence to score a parenting point.
jokes.explanation: Harold's dead-friends formula applied to cultural icons is funnier than when applied to anonymous friends. Jimi Hendrix as a cautionary tale about class-cutting requires willfully ignoring everything that makes Hendrix culturally significant. 'Choking on his own vomit' delivered with dinner-table calmness is the deadpan kicker.

jokes.text: Harold: 'You know who cut corners? KENNEDY. Kennedy cut corners when he was running the Bay of Pigs... You know who else cut corners? JANIS JOPLIN.'
jokes.setup: Harold escalates his lecture about Lindsay cutting class into a historical/cultural tangent.
jokes.punchline: The pairing of JFK and the Bay of Pigs with Janis Joplin as co-examples of 'cutting corners' is absurdist at its finest — one is a geopolitical catastrophe, one is a rock musician whose relationship to 'corners' is unspecified.
jokes.explanation: Harold's cautionary tale formula reaches its peak absurdity here. Kennedy / Bay of Pigs is a legitimate example of consequential corner-cutting. Janis Joplin is not. The word 'also' connecting them implies Harold sees these as equivalent examples, which is the joke. This is exceptional character comedy — Harold's logic is internally consistent (cut corners → die / fail), only the examples are deranged. Justifies 8+: original application of the running character joke, exceptional character integration (completely Harold-specific logic), and highly quotable.

jokes.text: Sam's concern at Lindsay's door: 'Do you think that me, Neal, and Bill could beat up Alan?' Lindsay: 'Just Alan?' Sam: 'Yeah.' 'Alan and his buddies?' Sam: 'Mm... no to maybe.'
jokes.setup: Sam visits Lindsay's room apparently to check on her. He leads with a serious question.
jokes.punchline: Sam's opening concern is a tactical bully-combat assessment, not emotional support. 'No to maybe' as a probability estimate for a physical fight is Sam's complete sincerity in absurd form.
jokes.explanation: 'No to maybe' is a great line — it's Sam's genuine probability assessment of a fight, delivered with complete seriousness. The moment is also funny because he's ostensibly checking on his sister but his actual purpose is fight consulting.

jokes.text: After Lindsay's genuinely moving story about their grandmother's death — the devastating 'there's nothing' — Sam: 'So... you do think that we could beat up Alan?'
jokes.setup: Lindsay has just told a deeply sad story about their grandmother dying alone, scared, with no vision of God or heaven.
jokes.punchline: Sam's response returns immediately to his fight assessment. The tonal whiplash — from profound grief to 'beat up Alan' — is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The best joke in the episode and one of the best jokes in the series pilot. Everything about it is earned: Lindsay's story is genuinely moving and given full weight; Sam's question isn't comedically telegraphed; the callback to his opening question is perfect; and Sam's response doesn't undercut Lindsay's story — it just reveals that Sam, at 14, has a very limited capacity to hold existential weight before returning to his immediate problems. It's both funny and emotionally true. The 'So...' before the question does enormous work — it implies Sam has processed the information and arrived at a conclusion. Economy is near-perfect. The structure is impeccable. Justifies the highest craft score in the episode.

jokes.text: Lindsay: 'Yeah. He's a goner.' — after the grandmother story, delivered with a small laugh.
jokes.setup: Sam has returned to the fight question immediately after the most emotionally heavy moment in the episode.
jokes.punchline: Lindsay's response — 'Yeah. He's a goner.' — matches Sam's energy perfectly, laughing at the shift rather than being offended by it.
jokes.explanation: Lindsay's response is the second beat of the best joke in the episode. 'He's a goner' delivers on Sam's question AND shows that Lindsay appreciates the emotional pivot — she can laugh at her brother's tunnel vision. The sibling relationship is communicated entirely in this exchange. Economy is nearly perfect.

jokes.text: Bill, pre-fight: 'My stomach hurts.' Neal: 'You'll be fine.' Bill: 'No, it really hurts. I think I should go home. This is how Houdini died.'
jokes.setup: Bill announces physical distress before the fight.
jokes.punchline: 'This is how Houdini died' — Bill's conclusion that his stomachache is literally how the famous escape artist died.
jokes.explanation: Bill's delivery of 'This is how Houdini died' is a standout joke. The logic — Houdini died from an unexpected punch to the abdomen, Bill has a stomach ache, therefore he is dying like Houdini — is both absurdly specific and internally coherent in a way that only Bill would construct. It's highly quotable, character-specific, perfectly economical, and deeply funny because Bill says it with genuine concern rather than as a joke. Justifies 8+.

jokes.text: Bill's ongoing pre-fight death lecture: 'You should always guard your face because if the other guy hits you in the nose, it could drive the nose bone into your brain. Then you'll release your bowels and die.'
jokes.setup: Neal has given bad pressure-point advice; Bill continues with nose-bone advice.
jokes.punchline: 'Then you'll release your bowels and die.' — the specific addition of bowel release to the death scenario is Bill's uniquely medical-disaster imagination.
jokes.explanation: The bowel-release detail is Bill's signature move — taking a scary fact and adding a humiliating biological detail. It's both more specific than necessary and funnier for it. This is part of Bill's pre-fight death-lecture series (jokes 75-77), which works as an escalating sequence.

jokes.text: Bill to Alan and Cindy: 'We're kind of here to beat you up.' Neal: 'BILL!' Bill: 'Well, we are.'
jokes.setup: Alan catches them on his street and asks what they're doing.
jokes.punchline: Bill's completely honest answer — 'We're kind of here to beat you up' — violates every social convention of covert bully-fighting. 'Well, we are' after Neal's protest is the second beat.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes and Bill's definitive character moment. Every other person on earth lies in this situation. Bill states the truth. 'Kind of' is the perfect qualifier — it's not 'definitely here to beat you up,' it's 'kind of.' 'Well, we are' after Neal's protest is a complete refusal to walk it back. This is Bill's entire character in two sentences: uncomfortable honesty deployed at maximum possible cost. Justifies the highest character-integration score in the episode. Economy is near-perfect.

jokes.text: The fight itself: Alan is beaten up by three small geeks who tear his shirt. Post-fight: 'You ripped my shirt! You're gonna buy me a new shirt! You guys are all dead!' — said while retreating.
jokes.setup: The geeks fight Alan — who they've been terrified of all episode.
jokes.punchline: Alan's post-defeat complaint is about his shirt. His 'you're all dead' threat while actually leaving undercuts any remaining menace.
jokes.explanation: The satisfaction of the bully's defeat is the primary payoff, but 'you ripped my shirt' as his injury complaint is nicely specific. The shirt was probably his good one. Empty 'you're all dead' threat while retreating is standard-but-earned bully-defeat comedy.

tmdb_overview: Lindsay's family and friends fear the worst when she quits the academic decathlon team and begins hanging around with a new crowd. A new friend tries to help her find her way. Meanwhile, Sam ponders asking cheerleader Cindy Sanders to the homecoming dance, while trying to cope with persistent teasing and torture at the hands of a savage bully.
